speaker: interviewee
text: Survivor's name is George Steinberg. The interviewer is Donna Kenny. We're having this interview in Long Meadow, Mass. Continue, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, who was the first person that you saw when you came out of hiding and you were liberated? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the names. Russian soldiers, on the first front. And he explained to me at that time if it's necessary, I shouldn't stay. Because when I had to raise my hands because this was the wintertime. And when I came to them and my wife and my child, I left them on the back end of the [? Shoah. ?] And when I came it was nighttime. Because the-- the first line from the war was at that time, at the time. And this was across from a Jewish cemetery, where I find. And I had to raise my hand. And when I came nearer with a-- with a gun, and told them who I am, I am a Jew. And I'm just going to-- coming out from-- from the hiding, and my family is in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he told me not to-- to still like that. And he went with me and take a look in the back, where my wife with the child was up there. When he saw them, then he made sure I'm not a spy or something. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And come on, come on. And he took us nearer where was there a soldier kitchen. They called it army kitchen, there. And I told him who I am. I am a Jew, and I just survive in Russian. Because the Russian language, I speak. And-- and he told me, sit over here a second. I'm going to [? write ?] we have with us a-- a Jewish soldier, a Russian. And he went and he brought it in. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And the soldier came in. And he looked at us, the way what we looked at that time, not dead and not alive. And he started to cry, because we had no tears to cry. And right away, he explained to me because I would-- I shouldn't stay. And I told him there is a flour mill and this is where-- belongs to-- to my wife. Anyway, it doesn't-- he said, don't-- and he told me right away, don't look at this. We came in with the front, like this. Maybe we can have some order to go back and straighten out, go to the back. And I took the-- and he gave me a piece of bread and everything. That's where we are in that time. Anyway, we start to go. It was winter and snow. We start to walk, and walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had him on my shoulders because you know? And we were walking. And you know, the way what we looked at the time, you can already-- and strength, I didn't have to walk [? with ?] [? myself. ?] Because I see in them how they are strong to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, and I saw a sled comes in. And he saw that some people with a child goes. And he stopped. And he stopped. And I see this Russian officer. Because when he-- red-- I see red marks on the [? pantsuit. ?] Didn't know the rank, what-- what kind of officer, but-- and I told him-- in Russian, I could speak-- and I explained to him, oh, yeah. And you give us the back seat. And he went in the front to the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] is the [INAUDIBLE] [? from ?] the sled. And we are on the way. And he said-- I told him and I showed the-- the history a little bit, of where I came. You know, this came out. And he was a decent man. Because-- and he said, you know, I came from a village and the neighbors, [INAUDIBLE] at that time, I mentioned the name because you like to have names too. And up there I saw some Jewish survivors. This was to be a good thing. And he took me there. Which I find there, some. I make sure of the story, when he brought me there. And I find that already some of them, they know them, and they know us. And [? they were with him. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found out that there were other survivors at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, then, to your family? What-- where did you live? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- where-- where I go? We went not to-- now, see, we went to the back from the war. Because the war was going on, in the back, not to stay on the front. Because we didn't believe they can-- the Germans can go back too. And we went, at that time, from there, from where I was liberated, not only myself. We was all in groups, some other people, some-- and we went to, in the first week, [? Rowne, ?] the biggest city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I will spell it the way you-- yes. [? A-R-W-N-Y. ?] Maybe it's not right. In the letters, what-- that's the name. Yeah. I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And up there we find, already, more refugees like myself. Because it was a country, you know? And-- and we were there a couple weeks. And had to go to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went out, went to another city. Not to go to the-- to the army. Because you know what it means for the army. We will be killed. [LAUGHS] And anyway, we weren't [? taken. ?] And somehow we came back to the little city where I came, almost. Yeah. And there, I made, already, some connections at that time. And with these people were there too. An then went to the army. I start to work in the-- in the back. They find me job and make sure [? there's ?] the story. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was, in the beginning, to make butter from the farmers who take milk. You ask me and I answer you, yes. And then I went from this job. Only the Russian officers gave me another job. Russian language, I could speak. This was also a help to my situation, what I was. And-- and if I believe otherwise, God gave me the common sense at the time. This common sense we had to use. And I use. And I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I left Poland? Yes. After a year and a half, maybe. Maybe shorter, not exactly the time. I had a farm. I told you. I mentioned there. I know that my-- and came to the Russian government, because our place was for Poland. But right after the war, they make Poland another area. And it was a other, not exactly other. You had to fill out the other farmers, Polish people. And if it's some Jewish people what they survived, they can go back to Poland. And they-- they will find there, their farms. I still have my piece of paper, what they gave me. And that time I went to-- to Poland. And I was in Poland only four weeks. I didn't came to the farm. Because my mind, and the mentality was, at the time, a different mentality and different scale in life. To go to the world, somewhere, where I can survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? This was on my mind. And I four weeks in Poland. And that time, when it became Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after four weeks, it's a long story. I made some documents. I'm not from the-- from this area. I'm from the German areas. It's a long story. Every detail, I couldn't explain. And it's not-- maybe not-- not necessary to know. And I went to Poland. Also, it's a history even how I-- I came there. But I came there. And-- and I was in-- in Poland. I told you already, four weeks. And then I make false papers. I'm a German Jew. And I went to Germany. I was five years in Germany, in the camps, DP camps, displaced persons camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp where you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- Pocking, in the fist camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pocking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pocking. Up there was, already, refugees like me. From all the area, from Poland, from Czechoslovakia, about 8,000, 9,000 Jews. Matter of fact, I have a picture what we demonstrate. And that time, we ask the world shall bring us to Israel. Israel was not liberated. And I have a picture that I took. And my picture there too, it does-- now, this is right here-- but anyway, this was my history from my life. And there I was five years in the camp, in the DP camps. And I had to go. My mind was only to Israel. Because-- but Israel was not liberated in that time. In the meantime, some-- my wife-- she'll rest in peace-- had some family here in Springfield. And somehow, it's not worth telling, find somebody was looking. And I sent a-- a letter. I still have same card, looking for-- for [? jobs. ?] He had some rooms, some from the family of the wife. And they send me papers. I make sure that the story to came to United States. And July the 2nd, 1949, I came to United States, and started my new life over here. And up till now, I had a decent life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in Pocking, at the DP camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I do? I was in the committee. We had, already, a [NON-ENGLISH], they call at that time. I was in the committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the committee do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The committee? At that time, they take care. It was 8,000 people. And-- I mean-- but-- anyway, I was already an officer. And I had, for this, a package from there at that time. Because UNRRA, the name, from the organization, for-- for certain things, they give packages for the war, for this. And they say, yes. Some cigarettes, and, you know, yes. But DP, displaced persons. They're making sure that doesn't-- cause DP.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a room? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: One room. It was barracks. The-- the Pocking, near Lage, was from soldiers. And there was wooden barracks, a lot. Because we were there, about 8,000, maybe 10,000 people in that time, in this Pocking, in the camp. And we had one room. Everybody has a room from the barracks. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Earlier you were talking about Aviva Feldman--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --whose parents were killed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and who had been placed with a gentile family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you found her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I brought her--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you found her, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I have papers. I adopted her after five years in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: You adopted her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have papers too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then we find out, because it was a little bit before her family, she left. She had an aunt, her father's sister, in Israel. And she find out. And sailed-- send a special-- it's-- I make this short-- shorter the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: For some it wasn't so easy to give up and here to leave us. Because by here we was you know, parents, and used to the same [INAUDIBLE]. We have pictures over here. And that's the way. And she's in Israel. Matter of fact, when my wife passed away, she called over here. She knows already. And a fine lady now. She is teacher now. She got too, married-- married, and in Israel. Fine kid. An officer, and had a decent life. And she's an architect now, herself. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you come by ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. General [? Albrook. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what port did you leave from in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: From--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --in Germany. Gerald, maybe you can remember? Nope. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right if you don't remember, Mr. Steinberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it have to come to my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it was Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- the time? No. I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, maybe it was Hamburg, Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I came to United States on July 2nd. And I left there about two, three weeks, we went with the boat. And the boat was General [? Albrook, ?] I mentioned already the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it landed where? In-- where did it land here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed in New York. And from New York, some couple give us tickets to come to Springfield, also with a recommendation from relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah. And I came up here with a train, in July 2nd. Yes. And I came in July. It's not necessary to this subject. Start-- I heard shooting. And we were scared people. And this was July-- before July the 4th. It's not necessary to mention. I'm only saying. yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you heard the celebration-- and the firecrackers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --going off, and that was scary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they explained it, the relatives. My wife had her cousins, and an aunt too, in Springfield. And they explain to us in that time the celebration for this holiday. And we accept the holiday, like any other patriots--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --up till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your occupation then, after you came to United States, and you settled in Springfield, Massachusetts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I came, or when I came?

speaker: interviewer
text: After you came to Springfield, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I came to them, July the 2nd. I settled by-- by my wife's uncle, with cousins. And my-- my wife's one cousin was electrician. And he worked with some Jewish-- they had a business from house to house. And he told him, you know, I just brought it over yesterday or day before yesterday. My cousin seems like maybe a businessman, maybe he have-- it's a job for him. And they said, maybe you send him to me. Make sure there's a story. And he send me to him, brought me to him. And I came there. And he talked Jewish at that time, to [INAUDIBLE]. And I explained to him where I am, and who I am. And he-- and he explained to me how to-- to go from door to door. And I told him, if I wouldn't be deaf, I'll know how to sell, if I'll be able to-- if they will understood me. And if not, then it's bad luck. And then I went and I started. And I was a certain time of my life with this, in the same business, and made a decent living-- living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked as a salesmen going door to door?

speaker: interviewee
text: From door-to-door salesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you speak other languages when you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I did it?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were selling, were you speaking other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the big help for me. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How many languages do speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to make a big man, seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And do you recall the name of the company you worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Superior Home Supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned, many times, your son, Jerry.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son, Gerald, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Do you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I find already, they find me a tenant to rent on Brooklyn Avenue, 44 Brooklyn Avenue. It's still there. And he was a school kid. Capable at [INAUDIBLE] elementary. Capable, and not only capable, not lazy. And wintertime, he went to clean snow and made a dollar. And the mother-- she'll rest in peace-- used to save his dollar in a separate-- yes. Then he went to school. He went to college. The rest of him, he will tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some. The best three ever, thank God. And a daughter-in-law, the best. You ask me, and I'll tell you. This my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been active in your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to make-- yes. I'm 30 years now, still there, there, the treasury. I paid my dues from the beginning. Because I wasn't such a-- yes. And up till now I'm still there, active, and do it for the shul, like any good believer in religion. Because I have there, in the shul, in the synagogue, I became-- I'm 30 years now, the Treasurer there, 30 years, took it over from somebody. And I have there a chapel, which is on the chapel, the name, and inside the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: The chapel is named for you and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was active in my shul up till today. Paid my dues regular, and [? beside ?] [? this. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Shoah often?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Shoah? Lots of time. [CRYING] This was my hope in my mind, and my wife, more, to leave some memories for the coming generation, what's happened to the Jewish nationality in the world. And if one place, as the United States, what they gave us, shelter, which we-- up to now. And gave us help for the Jewish nationality, what was left after Hitler, to help in the war too. And this is the truth. And this is the way in our mind, what we had. And up till now. And this is the truth. That's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: The future generation. This-- you asked me in my mind, I was a plain man. But in my mind, never again, [? disclosing, ?] never again to let-- to let what's happened in that time. And they saw, now, the history and everything, will help the future generations. Because the world, Judaism, is in the minority, not in the majority. And every minority suffers sometime. And every country different. And the United States is one from the-- the world, where they give shelter. The-- major from the Jewish population. And over here it's no different. You are human. You are good human. You are good. You are bad human, doesn't matter nationality. And this is what is my mind was in that time. And I still have in my mind before I die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any other statement that you would like to make before we end this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a lot of statement which I didn't mention over here, believe me. Because it was 10 years, took the time, from my liberation, until I came back to life in the United States, with a human feeling. Because before I came here, we didn't have human feelings. Now, we-- I don't have to tell you-- to explain to you, you know better than me. And yes, I have a lot of histories with connection even today, what I-- you talk to me today, you ask me today, a lot of stories. Because they 10 years, took for me, from the starting until the end, for my liberation. I was liberated when I came over here. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] If you have other questions, if I know it then I'll be glad to answer if I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have no more questions for you at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for your time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This concludes our interview, Mr. Steinberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice talking to you. God bless you. If I can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is rolling. Introduce your son, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to introduce him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son. His name is Gerald Steinberg. He survived with me from the ghetto and the foxhole. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Also, later on, the DP camps, for almost 10 years. And I came into the United States in 1949, with my family. This was my family, my wife, which she's not more any, here, and my son, Gerald. Now, thank God, my family became bigger. I have a daughter-in-law. Her name is Barbara Steinberg. She's here if you need her in the picture. And I have two grandsons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You want the names from the grandsons? The oldest one is Stephen Steinberg. He is an architect. He lives now in-- in-- I forgot the name. GERALD (SON): Buffalo

speaker: interviewer
text: In Buffalo, New York. Then I have another grandson. He was married, the middle one. His name is Michael Steinberg. And he married a wife. She is a doctor. And her name is-- help me please, I forgot-- GERALD (SON): Cindy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cindy. She-- and they both work in-- and he is also a doctor. They're both working in-- in Massachusetts Hospital now. Then I have a third son. His name is David. And he finished school. He liked to go in [INAUDIBLE]. And he is working in Springfield Hospital now, in the office. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You need some more?

speaker: interviewee
text: GERALD (SON): You did very well, pop. It is, indeed, a very significant occasion to have our family history recorded. [CRYING] [SOBBING] Although my father has grown in years, the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The story and the incidences are still very vivid in his mind. Although sometimes, he has trouble describing them. And hopefully, future generations will-- [CRYING] --be able to decipher these incidences so that perhaps such atrocities will no longer happen. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to talk a little about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try my best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is rolling. Go for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, could you explain to me please, who this picture is of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name was Faiga. The second name was Stejnseid. And that is-- is a different name because she was married, Stejnseid, with two children, with her husband. She was killed in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. GERALD (SON): Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] This was my mother. The name was Chaya Steinberg. Was killed in ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This was a farmer, Ukrainian farmer. What he took, and there-- there is relatives from my-- yeah-- two brother-in-laws, with his children. And-- and another brother. And they were killed. And this-- after I survived, I took, from the farmer, this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back row, the second picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The back row in the second picture, this is my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very famous man at that time, in that city. Not that I'm making up the story, but that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned two other relatives--

speaker: interviewee
text: yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on-- on the left, looking at the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. This was my brother-in-law. And this is children. And this is my wife's cousin. And this is the-- a son from my brother-in-law, a little kid too, right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture too, I picked up after I survived from farmer, what is over here in the picture. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture for myself and my-- my wife, before we were married. We had-- we had relatives in Springfield. And my wife sent to her aunt. Her mother's sister used to live in Springfield. Send her-- we are even not married at that time. And she sent this picture. And when I came here, they gave us back the picture. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll try to talk about this picture without actually touching it or pointing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was a-- a picture when I was in underground. I promised if I will survive, because my good friend, which he gave me shelter, came from the church. And he said, this is the-- the grave from the-- took-- the ghetto, where they killed the people there. And the dogs, taking out meat, you know, arm and legs, and brought it to him. At that time I said to God-- I talked to God, help me to survive. If I will survive, I will make a-- a picture, and then put it in a little stone, whatever I can do it, at that time. And when I survived, I kept my word to God, what I promised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, who are all these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the stone. And this is-- this is two candleholders in the side. This-- and the-- this-- the-- the plan from the stone, I was not an architect. But I did with my hand. And I took from a school when the Polish, start to build a school with the bricks. And I went to the-- to the Russian officers, and I ask, they should give me permit to give me some stones, for this reason, what I have in mind. And they gave me, the Russian, and they gave me stones. And this I made with my hand. And this is the two candles. Right this, next, it was my picture. With us [? over ?] [? here. ?] And this is survivors, what that survived. What I hear the news from the land because survived other people, not too many, but they survived. And this is the Russian officers, what that helped me to do this. And we made, in that time. And this-- this-- yeah, this is a Jew. And anyway, and this is Russian officers from the-- from the army. This is a general. They're not Jews, but Russian. But yeah-- yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without pointing, are you saying that the-- who are the figures in the very front, at the beginning of the picture standing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over here? Russian officers. And a couple Jews. This is the Jews that survived. And this is-- he survived only without his wife, only a child. Somehow he survived. And that's him. And this is a Jew who survived. And this is a Russian officer, big officers. They call them at that time, I don't know, politicians, officers that survived. One is a General, and you know, yeah. They-- they gave us-- and over here is a-- I don't know if I see them-- a Jewish soldier, a Russian. He made a big speech. And Russian, at that time, for the population. Because there was here, on the top, you see, there is some people, neighbors, what that live in this area where the grave is. They came to hear and watch the-- the-- the occasion, what it was. Yeah. And it was a real Russian soldier and officer. He made a big speech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the back, there were-- the rest of them with the speech, was other people, other nationalities, neighbors. But they-- they came in.
